chunk_summary: Guidelines for assessing breastfeeding adequacy in the first 14 days, focusing on feeding frequency, output expectations, weight loss, and red flags requiring intervention.
learning_objectives: Understand day-by-day feeding adequacy markers in the first two weeks of life.
Identify normal and concerning weight loss patterns in newborns.
Recognize red flags such as dehydration, poor output, and jaundice progression.
Learn appropriate follow-up and supplementation strategies for at-risk dyads.
key_facts: Newborns typically feed 8–12 times per 24 hours in the first two weeks.
Output expectations: Day 1 ≥1 wet diaper, Day 2 ≥2 wets and ≥2 stools, Day 5 onward ≥6 wets and ≥3–4 yellow stools per day.
Physiologic weight loss up to 7% is common; >10% or continued loss after day 3 requires urgent feeding assessment.
Most infants should regain birth weight by 10–14 days; failure to do so or daily weight gain <20–30 g/day warrants reassessment.
Red flags include lethargy, <8 feeds/day, scant urine/stool, fever, or worsening jaundice.
Post-discharge follow-up should occur within 48 hours for high-risk dyads (e.g., late preterm, post-cesarean, jaundice).
reference_quote: Loss up to about 7% is common; >10% or continued loss after day 3 warrants same-day feeding assessment (latch, swallowing, maternal milk onset) and a plan to improve milk transfer.
document_key_topics: Weight loss and regain patterns in the first two weeks
Red flags for feeding and hydration issues
